speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you can go ahead and slate it. Are we rolling? Yep. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slate, you're supposed to-- well, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll read.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's going to read it for us?

speaker: interviewer
text: April 21, 1996, Dr. Robert Fisch. Scott Bryce is the interviewer, Minneapolis, Minnesota, USA. This will be done in English. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Scott Bryce, S-C-O-T-T B-R-Y-C-E. Today's date is April 21, 1996. It is indeed my privilege and pleasure to introduce Dr. Robert Fisch. He will be interviewed here in Minneapolis, Minnesota. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Dr. Fisch, tell us a little bit about your, your childhood, your early days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I'm very appreciative to be interviewed because I think this has a-- very important consequences in future. But let's go back to your question. I was born in 1925, on June 12th, in Budapest, Hungary. And I had a brother who was-- is still six years older, fortunately. And my parents lived in Budapest. And they were merchants. Actually, they were in the poultry business. And they had a place when the-- these poultry come from the country, they worked it out, came to our shop. And we-- my parents sold it to customers directly or to restaurants and hotels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excellent, let's stop for a minute and first mention and spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: F-I-S-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other names that you've used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have a middle name, O, which is-- stands for Otto, O-T-T-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 70, soon 71.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Now, let's go back to your childhood. And tell us a little bit about not just your parents, but where they lived in Budapest and what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they lived in Budapest in the so-called XIX District, with Csarnok there. 'Csarnok' is-- mean marketplace, market square. And actually, they worked in this big, beautiful building which is-- still is there and actually is a magnificent building built in the last century. And it is mostly the conglomerate of different kind of food, so from fish to vegetables, flowers, and so forth. It's a magnificent big building. And it was a center at that time for shopping and for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents lived very close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, they lived really very close to the business place. And my mother was a very hardworking lady who worked from 3 o'clock in the morning until 7:00 in the evening on a weekday, and on the-- on Saturday, even 2 o'clock in the morning until 8:00. And they worked also Sunday, at least four hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was he a poultry salesman? Did he sell the poultry to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father--

speaker: interviewer
text: --people?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was also in the same business. But also in the fall to spring, they were involved in selling the-- they called it wild animals, [LAUGHS] which is actually forest animals, like rabbit, deer, and so forth. And the hunters brought pheasant and things of that nature-- wild ducks, and so on. And they'd bring it to, to our business. And that was partly also the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- your the father have a-- had a store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it is a-- you see, there was a-- you have to look at, like, almost like a-- currently, these big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like Roseville Shopping Center. But this was a food shopping center. So everybody had a certain area which was their store.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Now in, in selling to the people in the area, was it specifically a Jewish area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not at all. This was served for everybody, for every kind of workers. And, as I mentioned to you previously, they sold it for big hotels and restaurant business. So we know almost everybody. My father was very familiar with the whole population, which was not a small place. They were very well-known people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's you first memories, your youngest memories of your-- of, of going to-- with your father to this-- do this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think the first memories of that kind would be that because Saturdays, they couldn't even come home for noon, like, to eat. And someone had to take down the food for them. Then, every Saturday, because we didn't eat with them on that specific day, we have to go down early afternoon and stay there, kiss them, and wish them a good day, and so forth. That was my earliest memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever work with-- in cleaning chickens or--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I was just eating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --children. And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --my mother was a-- came from a very-- deprived circumstances. And she provided us every kind of opportunity, studying, language, music, and so forth. And she did not allow us to do any work because she want us to be-- and I had a different kind of life, what she couldn't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of music did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, violin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Violin. And you still play?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] No, no, thanks God, I don't. [LAUGHS] But I enjoy very much classical music.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When you were young, you had a brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my brother is still the-- my brother-- we will probably talk about this later-- because he-- we were Jewish, we could not go to the university. The university had, in Hungary, so-called numerus clausus. 'Numerus clausus' is a Latin word for exclusion by number. And it was a very limited number of Jews could go to the university.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was always very interested in engineering. And my parents were able to send him to Switzerland. So during-- he was studying in Zurich, which was at that time in Europe one of the best-known engineering schools-- still is in Europe. And he was there, fortunately, during the whole period, which was very fortunate because his age group of individuals who stayed in Hungary mostly were killed or taken to camps, and so forth. Did you learn German, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we learned-- we learned Hebrew. We learned German. We learned French. And first of all, we learned Latin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is the language, of course, at your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, and naturally, [LAUGHS] Hungarian, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. When, when you were young, what was your family life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was really structured very much around the work of my parents, naturally. And they were very much-- expected us to be available. And so we had a very precise schedule. My mother, in the-- during the weekdays, after they take a-- the eating, she took a half-hour nap. And so we were eating at 1 o'clock very precisely and 7 o'clock in the evening. And it was the-- then, of course, we had to study, which, at that time, I didn't enjoy too much. And on-- my grandfather actually had a-- built a little on-- I wouldn't say a synagogue, because that was invading a building. But it was a place when Jewish people congregated and prayed, actually even during the holidays, which was very close by. And so we went there on the holidays. But I also, as a student, went to one of the larger synagogues. And in the Sunday, I-- after my, my-- the parents were working, my father get up about 6 o'clock Sunday and about 8:00, they shut-- 9 o'clock, they closed the shop usually. And then he dressed up and whistled for me. And I'll never forget that because it was a very special whistle. It was [WHISTLES]. And then-- I lived on the third floor in the building. And I went down with him. We went to the amusement park or Gundel, which is the best-known Hungarian restaurant-- outside, in-the-open restaurant. Then there, we ate-- we drink, we eat there-- it was close to the zoo-- and then came home and ate the lunch. And we went to either a theater, or something like that. And then-- that was more or less about our existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your father-- you and your father and brother? Did your mother come?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was not as free-- my mother usually came in the afternoon or later. But in the morning, she didn't. And my brother was older. So this mostly happened at the time when he was already out of the country. This is what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was born in 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1919. So he was a good bit older.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was about some six-- some years older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes, yes. So religious life was very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the religious life was important. In, in Hungary, there are really no such a thing as Reformed Judaism. It was either Orthodox, or-- which is a more or less the-- over here, also called Orthodox. Or we called it Neolog, which I would be more or less on Conservative here. We used different tallis, tefillin. And I prayed every morning. I was very religious for a long period of time until about-- this whole thing happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were very young and you went to school, was it a religious school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the schools-- we went to the local elementary school. And later on, I went to a gymnasium. It's called Eotvos-- E-O-T-V-O-S. Eotvos was a very famous Hungarian physicist. And I went to a gymnasium. A gymnasium means that we were mostly prepared for different schooling. In other words, in Europe at that time, the-- most people entered the elementary school and had four years of the general school. And they had to-- went to some kind of tradesmanship. Then, it was-- other schools were-- it's called 'real,' R-E-A-L. And those are for engineering, architect, and so on. And it was in the gymnasium, the people who wanted to go do something different, like law or medicine. And we had-- I had, when I-- because my parents were very much in business and had little time to spend with us when I was a little boy, was-- a young lady came to us. She was, at that time, 18 years old-- her name is Anna-- who became very important in our life and even then and later. And she was a very devoted Catholic. And was she practically raising me. And so we-- I was exposed very much on other-- every Sunday, I was pushed to go to the [LAUGHS] church. And it was Friday in a synagogue. It was a lot of religion for me. But anyhow, in the school, we were together with other individuals. It was some schools, when religion-- let's say Catholics or Protestants or Jewish school-- but I was not in one of those. Well, during the daytime-- because in Europe and-- I don't know if it still is-- but at that time, religion and state was not separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the religious education was actually compulsory. So that during the religious hour, the Jews went in a different class and the Catholic another, and the Reformed, or Lutheran, went to another. That was the only separation. One hour a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: More than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two hours a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What was Anna's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tatrai, T-A-T-R-A-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she lived with us. And actually, after-- I will probably talk with-- about her later, that during the war, my mother was hiding in her family. So that she-- they saved my mother. And after the war, she came-- she was actually part of our business with my mother, worked in the business. And after my mother left the country, she and I lived together. And when I left after the revolution, we have the home for her. So she maintained it. And whenever I went back, I find everything in the same drawer, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she was there with you, did she-- you mentioned she took you to the Catholic church on Sunday?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: --every Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: --take you other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, of course she did. We went to the-- when I was so small, we went to the parks and-- you know, in the morning and the evening-- and, and afternoon, I mean. And she walked with me. And she taught me a lot of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents object to you going to the Catholic church?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my, my mother was very much-- maintained the religion at home. But it was no objection at all. I think our home was very open for other people as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- the district you're living in you mentioned wasn't particularly Jewish. Did you find any anti-Semitism as you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that not in the home and the home environment. In the school, it was some evidence of it, unfortunately. And it was-- you know, it's-- it-- it's, it's hard for people here to understand that from birth on, you had identified yourself with a religion. And that was written on your birth certificate, in the school, on your indexes, on your-- every documentation. After your name and location, the religion was there. This is, of course, has a great deal of implication in-- later on. But-- so it was-- in the school, everybody know, naturally, what religion you are because you went to a different religious class. And let's say, if there's fights-- some taking place, and you would hit someone then, then maybe they'd say, you dirty Jew, or something like that in order to somehow try to appear to be more important or something different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you protect yourself by going around with other Jews or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not necessarily. I didn't look for preference on the base of religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the others? Did the other religions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I mean, I, I, you know, I would assume that maybe some people do that way. But I never was-- made ever a major concern when I find my persons. I think that's one of the tragedy of peoples. They're looking some common denominator, which identify themselves being white, black, Jew, or Christian, rather the quality of the people I want to associate with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we played on [LAUGHS] in the parks--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the parks.

speaker: interviewee
text: --more than on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, you would go out to the parks in the afternoon after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh, after-- when it was no school, and I finished my homework. But then I was, of course, little, and every day at least twice. It was very interesting, you know, that-- I think this is-- I was very fortunate to be exposed to certain different kind of thinking. And, you know, we've been talking about that Latin is a dead language, so to speak. And what a waste to learn and study something of the past. I think that, in order to understand our current situation and hoping for a better future, you have to know your past. And, and the Latin was-- although I really don't know more about it too much than I can read naturally the numbers or some of the things in the churches what is written on-- but it was a very, very important influence on me. For example, Lucius [? Severus ?] was one of the Roman senators. And, at one point, there was a rebel in one of the area in that time in Italy-- which was, of course, not called Italy at that time-- and Rome. And what it was, the, the people, the so-called plebs, which is nobility versus the plebs-- rebelled. And this senator went to give a talk. And the talk was about that [SIGHS] a body was-- the different part of the body is upset about the stomach. The hands is supposedly made a lot of work. The feet had to go there. The mouth is chewing, and so forth. But the stomach doesn't seem to work enough. So they decided they're not going to feed their stomach. So-- and then, of course, the consequences is very obvious. I think this was a very good impact on me. I think that, to me, it is not-- the issue is that who you are and what you do, what-- you have a certain role. You could say that some of the role is maybe more important for one than for that other. But the whole thing is like a wheel-- go around. And so that we have a specific role in this world, regardless what we are and what our education or responsibility is. Some of them are more glamorous than others-- like in a air-- let's say air carrier. Obviously, the pilots has the glamor. But on the other hand, the people under the deck is-- without them, there's no pilot can fly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Exactly. When you were a, a child growing up, you had, of course, what would be called a Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that associated with the synagogue or the small synagogue that your uncle built? Tell me about that synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It, you know, that-- I was, as I mentioned to you, very religious. And every Friday, I went to service in the evening. Especially, I even went to school with a beautiful building in Budapest, which is-- I think Tony Curtis is remodeling. It was the Dohany Street, which is one of the most famous synagogues in Budapest. In the school, I went there. And it was my, my parents' friends, some of-- most of them were Jewish by circumstances, but not by choice. So we had other people as well. And, of course, my teachers, with the exception of religion teacher, was not Jewish at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Jewish school, in the synagogue, you went Saturdays?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. I just went only to service at the very beginning. But I was, you know, I was not going to Saturdays except the rare circumstances. But I prayed at home a great deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. When you were growing up and going into the gymnasium, was this a big change for you, with different students, different people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was Well, it was different students, but I-- didn't matter to me one way or the other. It was-- you know, it was-- as the child, you adapt, and [LAUGHS] you have to go-- keep going. If he-- my, my-- it was interesting, you know. My mother never accepted anything except-- you know, she told to us she worked for us so hard. If I did something well, then it was the way it should be. And if I [LAUGHS] didn't, [LAUGHS] I get punishment, simple like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] When-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: The languages you spoke at home were-- Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarian. You didn't speak any Yiddish or Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not at all. My parents spoke German very well. But we did not speak Yiddish. My mother eventually went to Israel after the war, of course, and emigrated to Israel. And then, she's-- she know how to speak a little Yiddish. But at home, we didn't speak it. We spoke Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you preparing in, in school for a particular occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] occupation is-- I, I was a very good boy. You couldn't believe it, but I was. And Anna, was when they-- they asked me, when I was six years old, what are you going to be? And I said, Anna said I will be a doctor. [LAUGHS] But I [LAUGHS] didn't have any inclination one way or the other. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you focused yourself more in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I really didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: --school?

speaker: interviewee
text: --have any plan. My brother was very much influenced by a person at very early age to be an engineer. But I wasn't. I didn't have any specific desire. Actually, it was many different thought brought up from-- of them was that I should go in the hotel business. Hotel business in, in Switzerland was a school when they teach you-- and in France-- when they teach you for four years after you're finishing high school four different languages and then business and administration, cooking, and so forth. And it was a consideration I might go there. But I really didn't care to. And I, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there specific rituals, religious rituals at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think, you know, we kept all the holidays, Passovers and Yom Kippurs, after and, you know, previous evening and following evening. Oh yes, we kept it. And I always have a lot of fun with it. [LAUGHS] My father always mixed up which matzahs [LAUGHS] has to be put where. [LAUGHS] Yes, but we did-- kept religion. I prayed every mealtime for the bread. And, and I used a little yarmulke, you know, those hat, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of-- what kind of literature would be found in your home, books, magazines--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had many different books and, of course, newspapers and-- book-- my brother was very, very interested. We had a big library, actually, at home. And it was available, you know, literature from Greece, and-- Homers and English Shakespeare, and all this there. But I was very interested in Hungarian poetry and literature as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm very interested in the statement you made earlier, that there was very little anti-Semitism in the parks, some in school, very little around your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. I mean, maybe I was exposed to very little anti-Semitism. We're talking about right now when I grew up as a young child, or even in the high school. Eventually, of course, at the end of the high school years, this became more and more influenced by the outside world, and-- such as, like, in Germany, what's happening and Austria occupied, and so forth. At that time, of course, we became very aware of anti-Semitism. For example, that not only you couldn't go to the university, but also it's, let's say, the university student was walking in front of the school. And the-- and they started to scream, we want a country without Jews, and so forth! That was one of the slogans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that became obvious that anti-Semitism is, is-- exists. How much extent, I didn't know. But it became more and more obvious that we lived in a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of peculiar circumstances. The times we're talking about are in the 1930s?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was actually now in the early '40s. There, you see, the-- I was, in 1939, still in high school. I finished in 1943. And as you know, in 1939, the war started. And it was a lot of refugees. The Hungarians were very unusual in this regard. They-- for example, they let the Polish Army to escape through Hungary and actually went to Africa. Even so, they were on the side of the German. So we were exposed to, at that time, a little bit more of the reality of what's going on in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the time at which Adolf Hitler became chancellor of Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think I remember him when he became a chancellor. But I heard him many times to speak. And it's a-- it's a very interesting thing. And I would like to come back to this later because he was a very charismatic speaker that had an incredible impact on crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever talk about leaving or going to another country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. My aunt lived in United States since before the First World War. And it was a possibility that we may emigrate. But it was-- somehow not materialized. My father, for example, didn't want to leave the country. He felt very much he's a Hungarian. And, and he felt that he's a well-known person. And he is very-- for him, that was very important, rather than could places when he has to start all over again on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: A very, very difficult--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --decision to make, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that it was entertained many times, but it was-- never materialized.

speaker: interviewer
text: In September 1st of 1939, did you feel particularly threatened in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we better not do. We-- we're-- well, in '39, we, we didn't know exactly what is the situation, as far as future concern. I was very much listening to BBC almost daily for hours. And that was one of the most important things for us to get the news. But we certainly knew that there's going to be a very different world soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
